title: Scissor Lift / Pantograph — Mechanical Advantage and Geometry
content: A scissor jack uses crossing link pairs. Height h = n·L·sin(θ), width w = 2·L·cos(θ), n = number of scissor stages. Lifting force F_lift from horizontal actuator force F_h: F_lift = F_h·cos(θ)/(2n·sin(θ)) = F_h/(2n·tan(θ)).
extended_content: Electric forklift scissor mechanism: worm gear motor drives lead screw (horizontal actuator), converting rotation to horizontal compression/extension. MA increases as θ decreases (θ→0 → MA→∞ but speed→0). Speed: v_lift = F_h·v_h·cos(θ)/(F_lift) — velocity ratio. Minimum platform height H_min = n·L·sin(θ_min) at θ_min ≈ 10°. Maximum height at θ_max = 80°. Load capacity: structural analysis of link pin shear, bearing stress at pivot. Common mini-forklift: 1–5 kg load, 2-stage scissor, lead screw 2 mm pitch, 100 RPM motor → 0.2 mm/s lift.
units: h: m, L: m (link half-length), θ: rad, F: N
source: Norton, Machine Design, 5th ed.; Dunlop & Hartley, Mechanisms & Machine Theory; Mabie & Reinholtz, Mechanisms and Dynamics of Machinery
